topics.title: Real-World System Design Cases
topics.description: System design interviews test your ability to architect large-scale systems under real constraints: Twitter feed (fan-out vs pull), WhatsApp messaging (delivery guarantees, presence), Uber surge pricing (geospatial + real-time pricing). These cases reveal common patterns: denormalization for reads, event-driven pipelines for scale, caching strategies, and the fundamental pull vs push trade-off.
topics.story: A junior engineer at Twitter once asked: 'Why can't we just query SELECT * FROM tweets WHERE user_id IN (following_list) ORDER BY created_at LIMIT 20? That's just SQL.' The answer: with 300M users, some celebrities have 50M followers. Inserting one tweet would require writing to 50M rows instantly — a 'thundering herd' on the write path. Twitter splits users into celebrities (pull model) and normal users (push/fan-out on write). The humble SQL query became a distributed systems architecture lesson.
topics.analogy: Fan-out on write (push): when you post, write to every follower's feed immediately. Fast reads, expensive writes. Good for most users. Fan-out on read (pull): read all following users' tweets at query time and merge. Cheap writes, expensive reads. Good for celebrities. Hybrid: push for normal users, pull for celebrity tweets, merge at read time. WhatsApp delivery: store-and-forward with delivery receipts (single tick, double tick, blue tick). Uber surge: geohash grid + real-time supply/demand ratio per cell → surge multiplier.
topics.memoryAnchor: Twitter feed: precomputed timeline (Redis sorted set per user, score=timestamp). Fan-out worker writes to all follower timelines on tweet creation. Celebrities: pull at read time and merge. WhatsApp: messages stored on server until ACK. WebSocket for online users, push notification for offline. End-to-end encryption: keys stored on device. Uber surge: geohash cells (1km²), supply = drivers available, demand = open ride requests. Surge = demand/supply ratio → pricing model. Redis pub/sub for real-time driver location updates. Rate limiter: token bucket (allow burst) vs leaky bucket (smooth output).
topics.firstPrinciples: Twitter scalability: a read-heavy social network has N writes but N×following_count read amplifications. Precomputing timelines trades write cost (fan-out) for O(1) reads. The 50M follower celebrity problem: fan-out would write 50M rows per tweet — solved by hybrid pull for high-follower users. WhatsApp: 100B messages/day, need delivery guarantees. Each message has a server-side ID. Client ACKs receipt; if no ACK within TTL, message is retried. Uber: geospatial real-time — drivers ping location every 4 seconds. Geohash maps GPS to grid cell. Supply/demand per cell drives the surge multiplier. Key insight: surge pricing is a demand signal, not a profit maximizer — it incentivizes more drivers to join.
topics.bruteForce: Twitter: SELECT t.* FROM tweets t JOIN follows f ON t.user_id = f.followed_id WHERE f.follower_id = :me ORDER BY t.created_at DESC LIMIT 20 — joins millions of rows, no caching, unscalable. WhatsApp: send message → write to DB → recipient polls every second (polling) — high latency, server overload. Uber: scan all drivers within 50km for each request — O(N) geospatial scan per request.
topics.optimizedApproach: Twitter: Redis sorted set per user: ZADD timeline:userId score=timestamp memberId=tweetId. Fan-out service writes to all follower timelines on new tweet. Read: ZRANGE timeline:userId -20 -1 WITHSCORES then mget tweet data. WhatsApp: WebSocket connection kept alive per client. Kafka for message queue. Server stores until ACK, then deletes. Uber: Geohash index: encode driver GPS to 6-char geohash (1.2km² cell). Driver pings → update geohash. User request → query same geohash + adjacent cells. Redis GEOADD/GEORADIUS for proximity queries.
topics.whenToUse: Fan-out on write: social feeds with moderate follower counts (<100k). Fan-out on read: analytics dashboards, low-frequency reads. Hybrid: Twitter/Instagram with celebrity accounts. Geohash: any proximity-based feature (nearby restaurants, driver matching, location sharing). Store-and-forward: messaging apps needing delivery guarantees across intermittent connectivity.

examples.title: Twitter Feed Architecture Decision
examples.explanation: The pull model fails because joins on large tables at query time can't meet P99 < 100ms at Twitter scale. The push model (fan-out on write) solves the read problem but creates write amplification for celebrities. The hybrid solution maintains the precomputed timeline for normal users (push) and merges celebrity tweets at read time (pull). Kafka decouples tweet creation from fan-out workers, allowing horizontal scaling of the fan-out pipeline.
examples.realWorldUse: Instagram uses a similar hybrid approach. LinkedIn's feed uses a pull model with aggressive caching. TikTok's recommendation feed is different: it doesn't use social graph fan-out — it uses a real-time ML model to select content, eliminating the fan-out problem entirely at the cost of algorithmic complexity.

problems.title: Fan-Out Write Count Calculator
problems.description:
Given a list of users and their follower counts, and a batch of tweets, calculate the total number of Redis ZADD write operations needed for fan-out on write. Users with follower count > 100,000 are celebrities and are NOT fanned out.

Input format:
- First line: N (number of users)
- Next N lines: `userId followerCount`
- Next line: T (number of tweets)
- Next T lines: `tweeterId` (the user who tweeted)

Output: total ZADD operations.

Sample input:
```
4
1 50
2 200000
3 30
4 1000
5
1
2
3
4
1
```
Expected: `1130` (50+0+30+1000+50)
problems.hint1: Store follower counts in a Map<Integer, Integer>. For each tweet, look up the tweeter's follower count.
problems.hint2: If followerCount > 100000 (celebrity), add 0 to total. Otherwise add followerCount.
problems.editorial: This models the core fan-out on write cost. For each tweet, the fan-out service writes to every follower's Redis timeline. Total write ops = sum of follower counts for non-celebrity tweeters. Celebrities (>100k followers) are excluded — their tweets are pulled at read time instead. This shows why the celebrity threshold is a critical architectural decision.

problems.title: Uber Geohash Cell Assignment
problems.description:
Simplified geohash: divide the map into a 10×10 grid. Each cell is identified by `row_col` (0-indexed). Given a GPS coordinate (x, y) where 0 <= x,y < 100, assign it to a cell using integer division by 10.

Read N driver locations, then M ride requests. For each ride request, find how many drivers are in the SAME cell or adjacent cells (8-directional neighbors including diagonals).

Input:
- First line: N
- Next N lines: `driverX driverY`
- Next line: M
- Next M lines: `requestX requestY`

Output: for each request, print the count of nearby drivers.

Sample input:
```
3
15 25
16 28
55 65
2
12 22
50 60
```
Expected:
```
2
1
```
problems.hint1: Convert (x, y) to cell (row, col) = (y/10, x/10). Build a Map<String, Integer> of cell -> driver count.
problems.hint2: For each request, compute its cell then check all 9 cells (row±1, col±1) including itself.
problems.hint3: Sum the driver counts for all valid neighboring cells (clamp 0..9).
problems.editorial: Real geohash encodes GPS coordinates into a base-32 string. Prefix length controls precision (6 chars ≈ 1.2km²). This problem simplifies to a 10×10 grid where each cell is 10×10 units. The adjacency check (3×3 neighborhood) handles the edge case where a driver is in a neighboring cell just across the cell boundary. Uber uses this to find available drivers quickly without a full distance scan.

problems.title: Message Delivery State Machine
problems.description:
Simulate WhatsApp's message delivery states. Each message transitions through: `SENT → DELIVERED → READ`. Events arrive as `messageId EVENT`.

Events: `SENT`, `DELIVERED`, `READ`.

Messages can only advance forward (SENT→DELIVERED→READ). Ignore invalid transitions.

After all events, print each message's final state sorted by messageId.

Sample input:
```
5
msg1 SENT
msg2 SENT
msg1 DELIVERED
msg2 DELIVERED
msg2 READ
msg1 SENT
```
Expected:
```
msg1: DELIVERED
msg2: READ
```
problems.hint1: Define state order: SENT=1, DELIVERED=2, READ=3. Store current state as int per messageId.
problems.hint2: On each event, only update if the new state > current state.
problems.hint3: Print sorted by key at the end.
problems.testCases:
[{"input":"5
msg1 SENT
msg2 SENT
msg1 DELIVERED
msg2 DELIVERED
msg2 READ
msg1 SENT","expectedOutput":"msg1: DELIVERED
msg2: READ"},{"input":"2
m1 SENT
m1 READ","expectedOutput":"m1: READ"},{"input":"1
m1 DELIVERED","expectedOutput":"m1: DELIVERED"}]
problems.sampleInput:
5
msg1 SENT
msg2 SENT
msg1 DELIVERED
msg2 DELIVERED
msg2 READ
msg1 SENT
problems.editorial: WhatsApp's delivery state machine is append-only — messages only advance forward. The SENT event that arrives after DELIVERED for msg1 is ignored (backward transition). In the real system, these events are triggered by ACKs: SENT = server received and stored. DELIVERED = recipient device downloaded. READ = recipient opened. This state drives the single tick (✓), double tick (✓✓), and blue tick (✓✓ blue) indicators.
